title: research_1.3.1.1
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 3]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:** None are presented in explicit equation form.  However the description of steam/CO2 system condensation behavior suggests that partial pressure relationships govern the steam condensation temperature.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 2: Condenser Pressure vs. Thermal Cycle Efficiency**
        *   *X-axis:* Condenser Pressure [bar], ranging from 0.02 to 0.1.
        *   *Y-axis:* Thermal Cycle Efficiency [%], ranging from 64.5 to 67.0.
        *   *Trend:* The graph shows a curve that initially increases sharply with increasing condenser pressure, peaks around 0.04-0.05 bar, and then decreases relatively slowly as pressure increases further.
        *   *Key Data Points:* Maximum efficiency occurs around 0.04 to 0.05 bar condenser pressure. Efficiency is much lower(65%) at high pressure than 66.5% at lower pressures.
        *   *Conclusion:* The optimum condenser pressure for maximizing thermal cycle efficiency exists within a limited range (around 0.04-0.05 bar, based on the figure), because too high or too low pressures results in lower efficiency.

3.  **Tables:** No tables are present on the page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 1: Principle flow scheme of Graz Cycle power plant**
        *   *Description:* This is a schematic diagram of the S-Graz cycle. It shows the main components and the flow of working fluids.
        *   *Components, with inlet/outlet conditions:*
            *   Combustor: inlet 40 bar, exit 1400°C for a gas with 74% steam, 25.3% CO2, 0.5% O2 and 0.2% N₂ from combining a fuel (methane) and O2 coming in at 40bar, along with steam feeding the burners
            *   High Temperature Turbine (HTT): inlet 1.053 bar, 579°C, outlet is not shown.
            *   High-Pressure Turbine (HPT): steam at the inlet 180 bar, 565°C, outlet 600°C
            *   Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG): inlet 180 bar, 565°C, outlet 0.043 bar pressure to LPT
            *   Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT)
            *   Condenser: inlet from the low pressure turbine, exits remove H2O at Cond. P. and CO2
            *   Deaerator: removes CO2
            *   Feed Pump: moves the flow to the HRSG through the deaerator
            * Compressors (C1/C2)
            * Compressors (C3/C4)
        *   *Flows:* The diagram illustrates the cyclical flow of the working fluid through compressors, combustor, turbines, condenser, and heat recovery components. It shows how the heat of exhaust gas from the HTT is used to generate steam for the HPT and LPT.

title: research_1.3.1.1
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 4]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   The equation for net efficiency is:
    
        *   `n_net = ((P_T - P_C) * n_m * n_gen * n_tr - P_aux - P_O2 - P_CO2) / Q_zu`
        
        Where:
        * P_T is the total turbine power
        * P_C is the total compression power
        * n_m is the mechanical efficiency
        * n_gen is the generator efficiency
        * n_tr is the transformer efficiency
        * P_aux is the auxiliary power
        * P_O2 is the power for oxygen generation and compression
        * P_CO2 is the power for CO2 compression
        * Q_zu is the total heat input

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   There are no graphs or charts on this page.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   **Table 1: Graz Cycle power balance**

    | Parameter                                                 | Methane fired Graz Cycle | Graz Cycle with syngas at 150°C | Graz Cycle with syngas at 500°C |
    | :-------------------------------------------------------- | :----------------------- | :--------------------------- | :--------------------------- |
    | HTT power \[MW]                                          | 119.4                   | 115.7                       | 119.7                       |
    | Total turbine power P<sub>T</sub> \[MW]                     | 141.8                   | 138.0                       | 143.0                       |
    | Total compression power P<sub>C</sub> \[MW]                 | 46.5                    | 43.0                        | 43.6                        |
    | Net shaft power without mechanical losses \[MW]           | 95.3                    | 95.0                        | 99.4                        |
    | Total heat input Q<sub>zu</sub> \[MW]                     | 143.8                   | 143.8                       | 143.8                       |
    | Thermal cycle efficiency \[%]                            | 66.3                    | 66.1                        | 69.1                        |
    | Electrical power output \[MW] incl. mechanical, electrical & auxiliary loss | 93.2                    | 92.9                        | 97.2                        |
    | Net electrical cycle efficiency [%]                     | 64.8                    | 64.6                        | 67.6                        |
    | O<sub>2</sub> generation & compression P<sub>O2</sub> \[MW] | 14.7                    | 11.5                        | 11.5                        |
    | Efficiency considering O<sub>2</sub> supply \[%]            | 54.6                    | 56.6                        | 59.6                        |
    | CO<sub>2</sub> compression to 100 bar P<sub>CO2</sub> \[MW] | 3.0                     | 5.0                         | 5.0                         |
    | Net power output \[MW]                                   | 75.5                    | 76.4                        | 80.7                        |
    | Net efficiency η<sub>net</sub> \[%]                       | 52.5                    | 53.1                        | 56.1                        |

*Summary:*

The table shows a power balance comparison for a Graz Cycle plant operating with methane and syngas as fuel. The syngas cases are differentiated by syngas inlet

title: research_1.3.1.1
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 5]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

*   There are no explicitly written mathematical equations in this extract. The analysis focuses on efficiency values and percentage point changes. Statements such as "the net cycle efficiency would reach only 53% instead of 53.8%" are numerical comparisons rather than equations.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Figure 3: Influence of HTT isentropic efficiency on net cycle efficiency**

    *   **X-axis:** HTT Isentropic Efficiency [%], ranging from 0.85 to 0.92
    *   **Y-axis:** Net Cycle Efficiency [%], ranging from 51.0 to 53.5.
    *   **Trend:** A positive, nearly linear relationship is observed between HTT isentropic efficiency and net cycle efficiency. As HTT isentropic efficiency increases, net cycle efficiency increases.
    *   **Key Data Points:** The graph indicates that increasing HTT isentropic efficiency increases net cycle efficiency of the cycle. As the HTT isentropic efficiency goes from 0.85 to 0.92 (or 85% to 92%), the net cycle efficiency goes from approximately 51.7% to 52.8% or 52.9%.
    *   **Conclusion:** Improvements in HTT (High-Temperature Turbine) isentropic efficiency lead to a direct improvement in the overall net cycle efficiency.

3.  **Tables:**

*   There are no tables in this extract.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

*   There are no diagrams or schematics in this extract.

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 6]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    The text doesn't directly contain any equations but mentions thermodynamic studies and losses expressed in bar.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    There are no graphs or charts on this page.

3.  **Tables:**

    There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 4. Scheme of condensation/re-vaporization:**

        *   This figure presents a process flow diagram (PFD) depicting a CO2 condensation and water re-vaporization cycle within a power plant.
        *   **Components**:
            *   HRSG (Heat Recovery Steam Generator)
            *   Condenser-Evaporator (integrated unit)
            *   LPT (Low Pressure Turbine)
            *   Condenser
            *   Throttle
        *   **Flows**:
            *   Working fluid from HRSG at 1 bar enters the Condenser-Evaporator.
            *   Steam flows from HRSG to the Condenser-Evaporator.
            *   CO2 from the Condenser-Evaporator is throttled.
            *   Condensed water at 0.3-0.6 bar exits the Condenser-Evaporator.
            *   A 0.025 bar stream exits the Condenser-Evaporator.
            *   The LPT receives a stream that is then processed in the Condenser.
            *   Feed water from the HRSG flows to the Condenser.
            *   Water exits the Condenser.
        *   **Conclusion**: The diagram illustrates the path of the working fluid and CO2 through the condensation and re-vaporization process. The integration of a combined condenser-evaporator allows for heat exchange between the condensing CO2 and vaporizing water, recovering a small gain from the compression step. The pressures shown are key operating parameters.

title: research_1.3.1.1
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 7]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   The text mentions an increase of net cycle efficiency by 0.8 percentage points up to 53.3%. This can be represented as:
    *   New Efficiency = Old Efficiency + 0.008,
    *   Where the new efficiency is equal to 53.3%

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   There is a schematic of a system related to a deaerator supplied with heat from CO₂ compression intercoolers (Fig. 5).
    *   The deaerator receives heat input from CO₂ compression intercoolers and also receives working fluid from a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG).
    *   Feed water goes to the HRSG.
    *  The deaerator appears to be connected to a Low Pressure Turbine (LPT).

3.  **Tables:**

    *   No tables are present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   Figure 5 is a flow diagram representing a deaerator system.
    *   The system includes:
        *   A deaerator.
        *   CO₂ compression intercoolers, providing heat to the deaerator.
        *   A Low Pressure Turbine (LPT) connected downstream of the deaerator.
        *   A Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG), providing working fluid to the deaerator (with an indication of a return pipe).
        *   The diagram indicates a flow path for heat recovery from CO₂ compression, which heats the deaerator to then drive a LPT.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 6: Schematic arrangement of turbomachinery for a 100 MW S-Graz Cycle power plant**

        *   This diagram illustrates the flow of working fluid and the arrangement of turbomachinery components in an S-Graz cycle power plant. The key components are:
            *   **Compressors (C1, C2, C3, C4):** Multiple compressors are used, likely to increase the pressure of the working fluid and CO2.  Speeds of 12000 and 23000 rpm are specified for some compressors.
            *   **High-Temperature Turbine (HTT):** Situated between the HPT and LPT. "Comb. Chamber Fuel, Oxygen" is fed to HTT.
            *   **High-Pressure Turbine (HPT) and Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT):** Conventional steam turbine components. Both turbines run at 3000 rpm.
            *   **Gear Boxes:** Located in between Compressor/Turbine to motor or generator shafts for speed adjustment.
            *   **Motor/Generator:** Connected to the LPT and HPT.
            *   **Intercooler:** Placed between compressor stages (C1 and C2) to reduce the temperature of the working fluid.
            *   **HRSG:** Heat Recovery Steam Generator recovers heat from the exhaust to produce steam.
            *   **Condenser:** Placed after the LPT to condense the working fluid.
        *   The diagram also indicates the flow of CO2 for separation and recirculation, with labeled streams for "CO2 From Condenser," "CO2 Separation," and "Main return flow after HRSG.". Speed references (rpm) are provided for some components.

1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   There are no graphs or charts on this page.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   **Table 2: Estimated Investment Costs**

        This table compares investment costs for a reference plant (assumed to be without CO2 capture) and a Graz Cycle plant (with CO2 capture for CCS) based on the specific costs of equipment. The components are: `Reference Plant Investment costs`, `Graz Cycle Plant Investment costs`, `Air separation unit`, `CO2-Compression system` and `Other costs (Piping, CO2-Recirc.)`. The scale parameters and specific costs for each component are displayed.

        *   Reference & Graz Cycle Plant Investment Costs: \$414 per kW of electric power (kWel)
        *   Air Separation Unit: \$1,500,000 per kg O2/s
        *   CO2 Compression System: \$450,000 per kg CO2/s
        *   Other Costs: \$100,000 per kg CO2/s

        The table highlights the substantial increases in investment costs for the Graz Cycle plant, specifically attributed to the ASU and CO2 compression.

    *   **Table 3: Comparison of equipment size for a 400 MW plant in terms of power**

        This table compares the sizes of components in conventional combined cycle (CC) plant and a Graz Cycle plant in terms of power. Units are in MW. The components contrasted are: `turbine of "gas turbine"/HTT`, `compressor of "gas turbine"/C1+C2+C3+C4`, `steam turbine/HPT+LPT`, `HRSG` and `Generator`

        | Component                         | Conventional CC Plant | Graz Cycle Plant |
        | --------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ---------------- |
        | Gas Turbine (HTT)                 | 667 MW                 | 638 MW           |
        | Gas Turbine Compressor            | 400 MW                 | 251 MW           |
        | Steam Turbine (HPT+LPT)           | 133 MW                 | 121 MW           |
        | HRSG                              | 380 MW                 | 365 MW           |
        | Generator                         | 400 MW                 | 500 MW           |

        The table shows:

        *   Gas turbine size is similar for both plants.
        *   The compressor size is significantly smaller in the Graz Cycle plant.
        *   The generator is larger in because it must power ASU and CO2 compression systems.

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:**

    *   There are no diagrams or schematics on this page.

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 10]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

*   There are no explicit mathematical equations given in their typical form (e.g., NOx = etc.). However, the table includes cost and efficiency data.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Chart:** Fig. 7 shows the Influence of capital costs on the mitigation costs (methane-fired Graz Cycle).
    *   **X-axis:** Capital Cost Ratio between S-GC and Ref. Plant [%]. This represents the capital cost ratio of the syngas-fired Graz Cycle (S-GC) to the reference plant.
    *   **Y-axis:** Mitigation Costs [$/t CO2]. This represents the cost to mitigate one ton of CO2 emissions.
    *   **Trend:** The graph shows a generally positive, nearly linear trend, indicating that as the capital cost ratio increases, the mitigation costs also increase.
    *   **Key Data Points:** The trendLine indicates that the increased cost ratio increases as mitigation costs increase as well.
    *   **Conclusions:** The graph indicates that the mitigation costs of a methane-fired Graz Cycle become increasingly sensitive at higher Capital Cost Ratios. It is also noted that a Range of ASU costs have an effect to the mitigation costs.

3.  **Tables:**

*   **Table 4: Economic data for methane and syngas fired Graz Cycle.**

    *   **Columns:** Includes data for "Methane fired ref. plant", "Methane fired Graz Cycle", "Syngas fired ref. plant", and "Syngas fired Graz Cycle".
    *   **Rows:**
        *   *Plant capital costs [$/kWel]:* All values are 414 except for the syngas cycle at 258.
        *   *Addit. capital costs [$/kWel]:* For Methane fired: 288 and 258 for syngas fired, comparing reference to Graz Cycle.
        *   *CO2 emitted [kg/kWh]:* Methane reference plant emits 0.342 with 0 emissions for methane Graz cycle. Syngas reference plant emits 0.583 with 0 emissions for syngas Graz Cycle.
        *   *Net plant efficiency [%]:* The plants vary from 52.5% to 58%.
        *   *COE for plant amort. [¢/kWh]:* The grazing cycle is higher than for reference plants for both fuels, and this is more pronounced with Methane.
        *   *COE due to fuel [¢/kWh]:* Methane values are 2.24 and 2.47 for reference and grazing cycle, respectively. Syngas values are 6.03 and 6.24, with the syngas cycle being more expensive.
        *   *COE due to O&M [¢/kWh]:* These values remain static between reference and grazing powerplants
        *   *Total COE [¢/kWh]:* The greatest Total COE belongs to the Syngas fired Graz Cycle.
    *   **Comparison:**
        *   *Differential COE [¢/kWh]:* Methane fired has a differential COE of 0.74 while syngas fired has a differential COE of 0.68.
        *   *Mitigation costs [$/ton CO2 capt.]:* Methane mitigation costs are 21.6 while syngas mitigation costs are 11.7.
    *   **Summary:** The table presents a comparative economic analysis of methane and syngas fired Graz Cycle power plants versus their reference plant counterparts. The Graz cycle has zero CO2 emissions and lower mitigation costs. The syng
